stem: A 28-year-old woman with episodic migraine without aura presents to her general practitioner. She has 2 attacks per month, each lasting up to 24 hours. Oral ibuprofen 400 mg taken at headache onset has not provided adequate relief in the past 3 attacks. She has no cardiovascular disease, no hemiplegic migraine and is not pregnant. She wants an acute-attack medication she can self-administer.

data_table.Examination: Normal neurological examination between attacks, fundi normal, BP 116/72 mmHg
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, TFTs normal; no imaging performed as no red flags

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate acute therapy?

options.text: Oral propranolol 40 mg twice daily continuously
options.rationale: Propranolol is a preventive option, not an acute treatment; it is added when attack frequency is 4 or more per month or attacks are disabling despite acute therapy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Sumatriptan 50 mg orally at headache onset
options.rationale: Triptans are first-line acute migraine treatment when simple analgesia fails or is contraindicated; oral sumatriptan 50 to 100 mg is a standard starting point in Australian practice with no cardiovascular contraindications. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Oxycodone 5 mg orally at headache onset
options.rationale: Opioids are not recommended for migraine because of medication-overuse headache risk and dependence; they are not migraine-specific therapy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Ergotamine 2 mg sublingual tablet at headache onset
options.rationale: Ergotamine has largely been replaced by triptans in Australia because of efficacy, safety and cardiovascular risk profile differences. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

options.text: Amitriptyline 25 mg at night every night
options.rationale: Amitriptyline is a preventive option for migraine, not an acute treatment, and would not address an individual attack. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Neurology)

explanation.summary: Australian acute migraine management starts with simple analgesia (paracetamol or NSAID) plus antiemetic at the earliest sign of an attack. If this fails or is contraindicated, a triptan is added as second-line acute therapy, taken early when pain is mild. Combination of triptan plus NSAID gives better relief than either alone for some patients. Preventive therapy (propranolol, amitriptyline, topiramate or CGRP monoclonal antibodies) is added when attack frequency reaches 4 or more per month or attacks are disabling despite acute treatment. Opioids and ergot derivatives are avoided.
key_points: Triptans are first-line when simple analgesia fails or is contraindicated
Take triptan early when pain is mild for best response
Preventive therapy threshold is around 4 attacks per month or disability
explanation.pearls: Triptans are contraindicated in coronary artery disease, uncontrolled hypertension, hemiplegic migraine, basilar migraine and pregnancy; they should not be combined with ergot derivatives within 24 hours.

stem: A 78-year-old man with chronic alcoholism is admitted from a nursing home with confusion and unsteady gait. He has been on a thiazide for years. He has had no seizures. He last ate normally 4 days ago and has been on tea and biscuits. He has no focal neurology. The medical registrar asks about the safe rate of sodium correction.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 110/68 mmHg, HR 88, Temp 36.7 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: Mildly confused, no peripheral oedema, mucous membranes dry, no focal neurology, no asterixis
data_table.Investigations: Na 112 mmol/L, K 3.2, urea 4.4, creatinine 92, glucose 5.0, urine osmolality 420 mOsm/kg, urine Na 50 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate sodium-correction strategy in the first 24 hours?

options.text: Aim to correct serum sodium by 20 to 25 mmol/L over the first 24 hours
options.rationale: Rapid correction in chronic hyponatraemia precipitates osmotic demyelination syndrome (central pontine and extrapontine myelinolysis); the patient is at especially high risk because of age, alcohol and malnutrition. (Source: KHA-CARI Hyponatraemia)

options.text: Give 3% saline 100 mL intravenously over 10 minutes then recheck sodium
options.rationale: Hypertonic saline is reserved for severe symptomatic hyponatraemia such as seizures, coma or severe encephalopathy, none of which are present; mild confusion in chronic hyponatraemia is not an indication for bolus therapy. (Source: KHA-CARI Hyponatraemia)

options.text: Aim to correct serum sodium by no more than 8 mmol/L per 24 hours
options.rationale: Chronic hyponatraemia (greater than 48 hours' duration) correction should be limited to 8 to 10 mmol/L per 24 hours to avoid osmotic demyelination, especially in elderly malnourished alcoholic patients. (Source: KHA-CARI / Australian Hyponatraemia Guidance)

options.text: Restrict total fluid intake to 500 mL per 24 hours without other measures
options.rationale: Fluid restriction is a tool but is unlikely to achieve 8 to 10 mmol/L correction safely in the first 24 hours when the patient is symptomatic; the thiazide must also be ceased. (Source: KHA-CARI Hyponatraemia)

options.text: Infuse intravenous 3% saline at 1 mL/kg/hour continuously for 24 hours
options.rationale: Continuous high-rate hypertonic infusion risks over-rapid correction; if hypertonic saline is needed it is bolused in small aliquots with frequent re-measurement, not continuous infusion. (Source: KHA-CARI Hyponatraemia)

explanation.summary: Chronic hyponatraemia (greater than 48 hours' duration) correction must be slow to avoid osmotic demyelination syndrome. Australian guidance limits correction to 8 mmol/L in the first 24 hours in high-risk patients (alcoholism, malnutrition, hypokalaemia, age above 65) and 10 mmol/L per 24 hours otherwise. Acute symptomatic hyponatraemia with seizures, severe confusion or coma is treated with 3% saline 100 to 150 mL boluses with frequent re-measurement. Over-correction is treated with cessation of saline and reintroduction of free water (oral or 5% glucose), or DDAVP rescue.
key_points: Chronic hyponatraemia: limit correction to 8 mmol/L per 24 hours in high-risk patients
3% saline bolus is reserved for severe symptoms (seizures, coma)
Over-correction: stop saline, start free water, consider DDAVP
explanation.pearls: DDAVP can be used to deliberately slow or reverse over-rapid sodium correction; it is given 1 to 2 micrograms IV/SC every 6 to 8 hours alongside free water replacement when correction has exceeded 8 mmol/L in 24 hours.

stem: A 58-year-old man presents with 2 days of pleuritic chest pain and dyspnoea. He returned from a long-haul flight 5 days ago. His Wells score is 6. CT pulmonary angiography confirms bilateral segmental pulmonary emboli with right ventricular enlargement. He is normotensive on arrival.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 112, RR 24, SpO2 92% in room air
data_table.Examination: Mild dyspnoea, JVP raised 4 cm, lungs clear, no peripheral oedema
data_table.Investigations: Troponin I 65 ng/L (cut-off 14), NT-proBNP 920 ng/L, CTPA RV/LV diameter ratio 1.2, ECG sinus tachycardia and S1Q3T3

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate management?

options.text: Discharge on apixaban with general practitioner review in 7 days from now
options.rationale: Outpatient management is appropriate for low-risk PE (sPESI 0 or Hestia criteria met), not for intermediate-high-risk PE with RV strain and positive biomarkers. (Source: THANZ Position Statement: PE)

options.text: Systemic thrombolysis using intravenous alteplase 100 mg over 2 hours
options.rationale: Thrombolysis is reserved for haemodynamically unstable (massive) PE or intermediate-high-risk PE that decompensates; routine thrombolysis in normotensive intermediate-high-risk PE does not improve mortality and increases major bleeding. (Source: THANZ Position Statement: PE)

options.text: Inferior vena cava filter inserted by interventional radiology in 24 hours
options.rationale: IVC filters are reserved for patients with absolute contraindication to anticoagulation or with recurrent PE despite anticoagulation; the patient can be anticoagulated. (Source: THANZ Position Statement: PE)

options.text: Therapeutic anticoagulation with low-molecular-weight heparin and admission
options.rationale: Intermediate-high-risk PE (normotensive with RV dysfunction plus positive biomarkers) is managed with therapeutic anticoagulation and inpatient monitoring for haemodynamic deterioration, with systemic thrombolysis reserved for decompensation. (Source: TSANZ/Thrombosis & Haemostasis Society Australia)

options.text: Surgical pulmonary embolectomy via median sternotomy on emergency theatre
options.rationale: Surgical embolectomy is reserved for haemodynamically unstable PE with thrombolysis contraindication or failure, or for large central thrombus; it is not first-line in stable intermediate-high-risk PE. (Source: THANZ Position Statement: PE)

explanation.summary: PE risk stratification drives management: high-risk (haemodynamic instability) gets thrombolysis or embolectomy. Intermediate-high-risk (normotensive with RV dysfunction plus positive biomarkers) gets therapeutic anticoagulation with close monitoring for decompensation, when rescue thrombolysis can be deployed. Intermediate-low-risk gets standard anticoagulation, and low-risk patients (sPESI 0, no RV dysfunction, no biomarker elevation) are eligible for outpatient management. Anticoagulation is typically a DOAC for at least 3 months in provoked PE.
key_points: Intermediate-high-risk = RV dysfunction + positive biomarker, normotensive
Anticoagulation with monitoring; thrombolysis if decompensates
Outpatient management requires sPESI 0 (or equivalent) plus social suitability
explanation.pearls: In the PEITHO trial, routine systemic thrombolysis in intermediate-high-risk PE prevented haemodynamic decompensation but did not reduce mortality and increased major bleeding and stroke, which is why current guidance reserves it for decompensation.

stem: A 64-year-old man with diabetic chronic kidney disease (eGFR 22) presents with weakness and palpitations. He has been on perindopril, spironolactone and ibuprofen for joint pain. He has had no chest pain. The triage ECG shows peaked T waves, prolonged PR interval and widened QRS at 130 ms.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/82 mmHg, HR 68, RR 16, SpO2 98% in room air
data_table.Examination: Alert, weak grip strength bilaterally, no peripheral oedema, no focal neurology, cardiac sounds normal
data_table.Investigations: K 7.4 mmol/L, Na 138, urea 22, creatinine 280, HCO3 18, glucose 11, ECG as above

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Urgent haemodialysis without any pharmacological pretreatment
options.rationale: Dialysis is the definitive treatment for refractory or severe hyperkalaemia in CKD but takes time to arrange; calcium must be given first to stabilise the myocardium while dialysis is mobilised. (Source: KHA-CARI Hyperkalaemia)

options.text: Intravenous calcium chloride 10% 10 mL via a peripheral cannula
options.rationale: Calcium chloride provides 3 times more elemental calcium than gluconate but is highly irritant to veins and is reserved for central venous access; gluconate is the preferred peripheral agent. (Source: KHA-CARI Hyperkalaemia)

options.text: Soluble insulin 10 units intravenously with 50 mL of 50% dextrose
options.rationale: Insulin-dextrose shifts potassium intracellularly but does not stabilise the myocardium; it is the second step after calcium when ECG changes are present. (Source: KHA-CARI Hyperkalaemia)

options.text: Oral sodium polystyrene sulfonate 30 g as a single dose
options.rationale: Cation exchange resins act over hours via faecal potassium loss and do nothing for acute ECG changes; they are an adjunct, not the immediate response. (Source: KHA-CARI Hyperkalaemia)

options.text: Calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL intravenously over 2 minutes
options.rationale: In hyperkalaemia with ECG changes, intravenous calcium is the first agent to stabilise the myocardial membrane, with onset within 1 to 3 minutes; it does not lower potassium but buys time for shifting and excretion therapies. (Source: KHA-CARI Hyperkalaemia)

explanation.summary: Severe hyperkalaemia with ECG changes follows a stepwise approach: stabilise the myocardium (calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL IV, repeat in 5 minutes if ECG unchanged), shift potassium intracellularly (insulin-dextrose, salbutamol nebuliser, bicarbonate if acidotic), remove potassium (loop diuretics if not anuric, cation exchange resins, dialysis). Withdraw culprit drugs (ACEi, ARB, MRA, NSAID, trimethoprim, beta blocker if relevant). Repeat ECG after each intervention. Dialysis is indicated for refractory hyperkalaemia, severe AKI, or when removal mechanisms fail.
key_points: Calcium first when ECG changes are present
Then shift (insulin-dextrose, salbutamol nebuliser, bicarbonate if acidotic)
Then remove (loop diuretic, resins, dialysis); withdraw culprit drugs
explanation.pearls: Calcium gluconate 10% provides 2.2 mmol of elemental calcium per 10 mL; calcium chloride 10% provides 6.8 mmol per 10 mL but causes tissue necrosis if extravasated, which is why peripheral first-line use defaults to gluconate.

stem: A 56-year-old woman has an incidental 2.8 cm right adrenal mass identified on a CT abdomen done for nephrolithiasis. The CT density is 5 Hounsfield units on the unenhanced phase. She has hypertension on amlodipine and irbesartan, no episodic symptoms, no Cushingoid features, and no family history of endocrine disease.

data_table.Examination: No striae, no facial plethora, no proximal myopathy, no palpable mass
data_table.Investigations: CT: right adrenal mass 2.8 cm, 5 HU non-contrast (lipid-rich); FBC, UEC, LFTs normal

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: 1 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test with plasma metanephrines
options.rationale: All adrenal incidentalomas are screened for autonomous cortisol secretion (1 mg overnight DST) and phaeochromocytoma (plasma or 24 h urine fractionated metanephrines); aldosterone-renin ratio is added in hypertensive or hypokalaemic patients. (Source: ESE/ENSAT Incidentaloma 2023)

options.text: 24-hour urinary 5-HIAA combined with serum chromogranin A measurement
options.rationale: 5-HIAA and chromogranin A screen for carcinoid and neuroendocrine tumours; they are not part of adrenal incidentaloma workup. (Source: ESE Adrenal Incidentaloma 2023)

options.text: Image-guided fine-needle aspiration of the right adrenal mass this week
options.rationale: FNA is contraindicated until phaeochromocytoma is excluded (catecholamine crisis risk) and rarely changes management for indeterminate adrenal lesions; it is not part of routine workup. (Source: ESE Adrenal Incidentaloma 2023)

options.text: Repeat non-contrast CT abdomen in 6 months without biochemical testing
options.rationale: Imaging surveillance alone misses functioning tumours; biochemical screening for cortisol and metanephrines is mandatory at first identification regardless of imaging features. (Source: ESE Adrenal Incidentaloma 2023)

options.text: Right adrenalectomy without further investigation given size and density
options.rationale: Surgery is considered for masses larger than 4 cm, indeterminate imaging features (above 20 HU) or proven autonomous secretion; this 2.8 cm lipid-rich (5 HU) mass requires biochemical workup, not immediate surgery. (Source: ESE Adrenal Incidentaloma 2023)

explanation.summary: Adrenal incidentaloma evaluation tests two questions: is it functioning, and is it malignant? Functional workup includes 1 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test (cortisol greater than 50 nmol/L suggests autonomous secretion), plasma or 24 h urine fractionated metanephrines, and aldosterone-renin ratio if hypertensive or hypokalaemic. Imaging features favouring benign include size below 4 cm, unenhanced CT density below 10 HU (lipid-rich adenoma) and homogeneous appearance. Surgery is considered for masses larger than 4 cm, indeterminate or suspicious imaging, or functional tumours. Non-functional, low-density lesions below 4 cm need re-evaluation as guided by ESE 2023.
key_points: Screen all incidentalomas for cortisol and metanephrines; add ARR if hypertensive/hypokalaemic
Lipid-rich (less than 10 HU) lesions below 4 cm are usually benign adenomas
Surgical referral for size above 4 cm, indeterminate imaging, or proven function
explanation.pearls: In an adrenal mass, an unenhanced CT density of 10 HU or less indicates a lipid-rich adenoma with greater than 98% specificity for benign disease, which is the imaging feature that allows safe biochemical-screening-only workup.
